name: MDS (Material Drying System)
description: The MDS (Material Drying System) is a filament drying enclosure designed and manufactured by Fracktal Works. It supports 1 kg and 3 kg spools, uses active heating combined with a replaceable desiccant chamber to reduce filament moisture, and integrates pneumatic filament feed-through to printers. The product is intended for use alongside Fracktal's printer fleet and is sold as an accessory. The current phase covers finalising manufacturing drawings, vendor quotation, procurement, assembly, and full functional testing — all within a one-week sprint owned entirely by Suryansh Lal. Reference: https://fracktal.in/materialdryer/

team.role: Project Owner — Design, Fabrication, Procurement, Assembly & Test

milestones.milestone: Manufacturing drawings complete & sent to vendor
BOM from CAD + ODOO naming convention updated
All components issued from inventory or procured
Test plan written and all tests passed

dependencies: Vendor turnaround for fabricated parts (sheet metal / machined parts) — critical path
Inventory availability for bought-out components (bearings, heater, desiccant, pneumatic fittings)
ODOO part master — must be accessible for Suryansh / Anirudh / Kiran to update naming

functional_requirements.req: Accept both 1 kg and 3 kg filament spools without modification
Allow spool replacement without tools in under 60 seconds
Desiccant canister must be user-replaceable without disassembling the unit
Heating system must reach and hold target drying temperature (per spec)
Pneumatic filament feed-through must route filament to printer without kinking
Electronics subsystem must control temperature and indicate status
Full unit must be assembleable by a single person following assembly SOP
No DFA issues — fasteners, access, and assembly sequence must be validated

constraints: Must be completed within 1 week (hard deadline: June 13, 2026)
Suryansh is the sole engineer — no additional headcount available for build/test
Components must be sourced from existing inventory first; purchase only what is missing
BOM naming must follow existing ODOO subsystem naming convention — no new naming schemes

personas.name: Printer Operator
personas.description: Uses the MDS daily alongside a Fracktal printer; needs to swap spools and desiccant quickly without needing tools or a manual

personas.name: Field Technician
personas.description: Installs and services units at customer sites (515 Base, Hisar); needs to diagnose heating and pneumatic issues in the field

architecture: The MDS is a standalone heated enclosure. A sheet-metal or fabricated main body houses the spool spindle, heating element, temperature sensor, desiccant chamber, and electronics. A pneumatic fitting on the side routes the filament out to the printer via a PTFE tube. The electronics board (temperature controller + relay or PWM driver) maintains the set temperature. The enclosure has a hinged or removable lid for spool access, and a dedicated desiccant port for quick replacement.
how_it_works: The operator places a spool on the spindle and loads the filament end through the pneumatic fitting into the printer's extruder. The heating element brings the internal temperature to the drying setpoint. The desiccant absorbs residual moisture. The printer draws filament through the PTFE tube during printing. After the spool is consumed, the lid is opened, the spool is swapped, and the filament is re-threaded — no tools required. The desiccant is replaced periodically by pulling out the canister through its dedicated access port.

design_decisions.title: Active heating + desiccant (hybrid drying)
design_decisions.context: Desiccant-only dryers are slow and require frequent regeneration. Heating-only dryers without desiccant are less effective at low temperatures.
design_decisions.decision: Use a resistive heating element with temperature controller combined with a replaceable silica gel desiccant canister.
design_decisions.rationale: Hybrid approach dries faster, maintains dryness during multi-hour prints, and the desiccant extends effectiveness at lower temperatures.
design_decisions.consequences: Requires electronics BOM, adds ~₹XXX to cost vs desiccant-only. Desiccant must be replaced or regenerated periodically.

design_decisions.title: Pneumatic filament feed-through
design_decisions.context: An open slot in the enclosure loses heat and allows ambient humidity in. A sealed port is better but requires the filament to enter through a fitting.
design_decisions.decision: Use a pneumatic push-fit coupling (PC4-M6 or equivalent) on the enclosure wall as the filament exit point.
design_decisions.rationale: Standard 3D printer pneumatic fittings are cheap, stock items, and provide a near-airtight seal around 1.75 mm filament.
design_decisions.consequences: Filament must be cut and re-threaded through the fitting when swapping spools — adds ~10 seconds to spool swap.

design_decisions.title: Single-engineer assembly target
design_decisions.context: Field technicians and operators may need to assemble or reassemble the unit without a second person.
design_decisions.decision: Design all sub-assemblies so a single person can hold and fasten them. DFA check is a mandatory test milestone.
design_decisions.rationale: Reduces support burden and ensures the product is serviceable in the field.
design_decisions.consequences: May constrain enclosure geometry — overhang joints requiring two hands to align must be redesigned.

subsystems.name: Spool Housing & Spindle
subsystems.description: Holds 1 kg and 3 kg spools on a central spindle. Lid or side panel opens for spool access. Spindle must accommodate both inner-hub diameters common across standard filament brands.
specs: Supports spool inner hub diameters: 52 mm (1 kg) and 55–56 mm (3 kg)
Spool replacement: tool-free, < 60 seconds
Spindle material: aluminium or steel rod with low-friction bearing or bushing
known_issues: Inner hub clearance for 3 kg spool brands needs validation during assembly test

subsystems.name: Heating System
subsystems.description: Resistive heating element (PTC or nichrome) with a temperature controller that maintains the enclosure at the filament-specific drying setpoint. Includes a thermal fuse for safety.
specs: Temperature range: 40°C – 70°C (adjustable)
Temperature accuracy: ±2°C
Thermal fuse / over-temperature cutout: ≥ 85°C
Heating element power: TBD (to be determined from thermal analysis)
known_issues: Thermal uniformity across large 3 kg spools has not been measured yet — test item

subsystems.name: Desiccant Chamber
subsystems.description: A removable canister or cartridge filled with silica gel or molecular sieve desiccant. Accessible via a dedicated port on the enclosure without opening the main lid.
specs: Canister replacement: tool-free via dedicated side port
Desiccant type: silica gel (colour-indicating) or molecular sieve 4Å
Estimated change interval: every 1–4 weeks depending on ambient humidity
known_issues: Access port geometry needs DFA validation — must be reachable with gloves

subsystems.name: Pneumatic Filament Feed
subsystems.description: PTFE-lined pathway from inside the enclosure to a pneumatic push-fit fitting on the exterior wall. Routes 1.75 mm filament to the printer's extruder.
specs: Filament diameter: 1.75 mm
Fitting type: PC4-M6 or equivalent push-fit pneumatic
PTFE tube ID: 2 mm
Seal: fitting provides near-airtight seal around filament during printing
known_issues: Re-threading after spool swap requires pushing filament through — verify ease of use in test

subsystems.name: Electronics & Controls
subsystems.description: Temperature sensing (NTC thermistor or thermocouple), temperature controller (PID or on/off relay), power inlet, and status indicator (LED or display).
specs: Input: 230V AC (India mains)
Temperature sensor: NTC 100K or K-type thermocouple
Controller: digital PID temperature controller with relay output
Safety: mains fuse, thermal cutout, earthed enclosure
known_issues: Final BOM for electronics to be locked once CAD is complete and ODOO naming updated

subsystems.name: Enclosure & Structure
subsystems.description: Main structural body — sheet metal or fabricated panels. Provides thermal insulation, structural support for spool weight, and mounting points for all subsystems.
specs: Material: sheet metal (powder coated) or equivalent
Must support combined weight of 3 kg spool + hardware without deflection
All internal corners accessible for cleaning
DFA: single-person assembly validated
known_issues: Manufacturing drawings in progress — vendor quotation pending

open_questions.question: What is the target drying temperature for each filament type (PLA, PETG, PA, TPU)?
What is the heating element wattage required to reach setpoint within 30 minutes?
Which vendor will be used for sheet metal fabrication and what is the lead time?
Are any components not in inventory that have long procurement lead times (> 3 days)?
Has the full BOM been cross-checked against ODOO stock? Any shortfalls?
Are all CAD component names updated per ODOO subsystem naming convention by Monday?

risks.risk: Vendor fabrication lead time exceeds available window (>3 days) — blocks assembly
risks.mitigation: Identify vendor day 1, confirm lead time before sending drawings; have backup vendor identified

risks.risk: Missing components not in inventory with long procurement lead times
risks.mitigation: Run full BOM-vs-inventory check on June 7 immediately after drawings are finalised; order missing parts same day

risks.risk: DFA issues discovered during assembly (e.g., no access for fasteners, spindle interference)
risks.mitigation: Suryansh to do a CAD dry-run / virtual assembly check before sending drawings to vendor

risks.risk: Heating system does not reach setpoint or has hot spots affecting large spools
risks.mitigation: Test early in assembly sequence; if thermal performance fails, investigate insulation and element placement

risks.risk: ODOO naming convention update not completed by Monday — blocks BOM submission
risks.mitigation: Anirudh and Kiran to start naming alignment in parallel with Suryansh's drawings; daily sync
